coredata.dc:title: An ultra-selective fluorescence method with enhanced sensitivity for the determination of manganese (VII) in food stuffs using carbon quantum dots as nanoprobe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Liu, Ling-fei
Sun, Huijuan
Han, Jie
Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Lizhen
Yang, Zhen-quan
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence method was for the first time established for the efficient determination of permanganate (Mn(VII)) based on sulfur, chlorine and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,Cl,N-CQDs). The S,Cl,N-CQDs was prepared via a fast and simple acid-base neutralization method. The fluorescence of S,Cl,N-CQDs could be dramatically quenched by Mn(VII) ascribed to a combination of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching. Under the optimal working conditions, the S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards Mn(VII) sensing. A good linear Stern-Volmer equation was obtained for Mn(VII) in a wide concentration range of 0.05–110 μM with a detection limit as low as 12.8 nM. The proposed fluorescence method also exhibits additional advantages of great simplicity, fast in analysis and low cost. The as-fabricated fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied for the determination of Mn(VII) in vegetable, cereal and tea samples with high accuracy. It is anticipated that the S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe could be further exploited to construct a nanoprobe device for Mn(VII) monitoring in complex food matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S,Cl,N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching
Mn(VII)
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Linearity, selectivity and anti-interference ability study of S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe
Sample treatment and analysis by the proposed fluorescence method
Analysis of food samples by ICP-MS
Detection of Mn(VII) by the proposed fluorescent nanoprobe
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed fluorescent nanoprobe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication procedure of S,Cl,N-CQDs and its application for Mn(VII) detection in food stuffs.
The TEM image of the as-prepared S,Cl,N-CQDs. The inset in the top-right is the particle size distribution histogram of the S,Cl,N-CQDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line), excitation spectrum (red line) and emission spectrum of the as-synthesised S,Cl,N-CQDs. The inset displays the photograph a of S,Cl,N-CQDs solution taken under a hand-held UV lamp. (B) The PL emission spectra with increasingly longer λex in 10 nm increments starting from 310 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the Figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Effect of response time on I0/I. The S,Cl,N-CQDs solution was prepared in PBS solution (10 mM) at pH 2.0. (B) Effect of pH on I0/I. The S,Cl,N-CQDs solutions (0.075 mg/mL) were prepared in PBS solutions (10 mM) at different pHs. (C) Effect of S,Cl,N-CQDs concentration on I0/I. The S,Cl,N-CQDs solutions were prepared in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 2.0) at different concentration levels.

The PL spectra of a S,Cl,N-CQDs solution (0.075 mg/mL) containing different concentrations of Mn(VII) (0.00, 0.05, 0.45, 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0, 7.5, 9.0, 10.5, 16.5, 37.5, 60.0, 80.0, 140.0, 180.0 and 300.0 μM) in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 2.0). The inset graph (top right) is the Stern − Volmer plot of I0/I as a function of the Mn(VII) concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of five independent experiments.

(A) The I0/I of S,Cl,N-CQDs (0.075 mg/mL) after the addition of 0.5 mM Mn(VII)/other possible interferences. (B) The I0/I of the mixture of S,Cl,N-CQDs (0.075 mg/mL) and Mn(VII) (0.5 mM) before and after the introduction of other potential interferences (5 mM). The error bars are calculated as the standard deviations of five independent experiments.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Mn(VII) (purple line) and fluorescence excitation spectrum (black line) and emission spectrum (blue line) of S,Cl,N-CQDs. (B) The fluorescence decay spectra of S,Cl,N-CQDs solution before and after the addition of Mn(VII) versus the life time. The fluorescence decay spectra were acquired at a λex of 380 nm and a λem of 466 nm. The concentrations of S,Cl,N-CQDs and Mn(VII) used for the experiments are 0.1 mg/mL and 20 μM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in the Figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The analytical performance for Mn(VII) detection by different methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: L-cys MnZnS QDs: l-cys modified MnZnS quantum dots.
SS-ET AAS: direct solid sampling electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Food samples analyses by the proposed fluorescence method and ICP-MS.

head.ce:title: An ultra-selective fluorescence method with enhanced sensitivity for the determination of manganese (VII) in food stuffs using carbon quantum dots as nanoprobe

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Postdoctoral research fellow on visit to School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence method was for the first time established for the efficient determination of permanganate (Mn(VII)) based on sulfur, chlorine and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (S,Cl,N-CQDs). The S,Cl,N-CQDs was prepared via a fast and simple acid-base neutralization method. The fluorescence of S,Cl,N-CQDs could be dramatically quenched by Mn(VII) ascribed to a combination of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching. Under the optimal working conditions, the S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity towards Mn(VII) sensing. A good linear Stern-Volmer equation was obtained for Mn(VII) in a wide concentration range of 0.05–110 μM with a detection limit as low as 12.8 nM. The proposed fluorescence method also exhibits additional advantages of great simplicity, fast in analysis and low cost. The as-fabricated fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied for the determination of Mn(VII) in vegetable, cereal and tea samples with high accuracy. It is anticipated that the S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe could be further exploited to construct a nanoprobe device for Mn(VII) monitoring in complex food matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Permanganate (Mn(VII)) is an essential trace element in the metabolism of all living organisms, including human beings, plants and microorganisms (Lemos et al., 2009). The biological functions of all living organisms on earth are closely related to Mn(VII). The Mn(VII) plays an important role in the function of brain, normal bone growth, nervous system, enzymes activity as well as carbohydrate and lipid metabolisms (Lin and Huang, 2001; Teo and Chen, 2001). However, excessive exposure to Mn(VII) is harmful to the central nervous, respiratory, cardiac and reproductive systems, causing neurological disorders, DNA mutations, irritability, hallucinations, depression and even Mn(VII) madness to occur (Lin and Huang, 2001). With this information, it can be seen that the benefits and disadvantages of Mn(VII) to a human body are dose-dependent, and it is of considerable importance to control the Mn(VII) content entering a human body.

Foods are the major source of Mn(VII) for human beings. The foods of vegetal origin, especially rice and wheat, were found to possess the high contents of Mn(VII) (Lemos et al., 2009). Thus, developing an efficient and accurate analytical method for Mn(VII) determination is highly crucial to safeguard the health of human beings. So far, various analytical tools, including flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) (Lemos et al., 2009; Qian et al., 2001; Tuzen and Soylak, 2009), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Chen et al., 2019; Fu and Shi, 2019), ICP-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Oleszczuk et al., 2007), catalytic titration (Xuezhi et al., 1999) and spectrophotometry (Ayele and Chandravanshi, 1990), have been developed for Mn(VII) determination. However, most of the reported methods are relatively time-consuming and complicated in practice, and some techniques suffer from narrow linear detection range, which limits the detection efficiency of these methods. Recently, the fluorescent nanoprobes (Deng et al., 2017; Gong et al., 2017a), which display great simplicity, high accuracy, good selectivity and remarkable sensitivity, were found to be highly efficient in the detection of heavy metal ions and have drawn considerable attention of the scientific communities.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with diameters below 10 nm, are the newest family members of the nanoscale carbons (Baker and Baker, 2010). The CQDs have gained much attention owing to their superior physical and chemical properties, such as excellent photo-stability, tuneable fluorescence emission, broad excitation wavelengths (λex), large Stokes shifts, favourable biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity (Bourlinos et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2013; Zhai et al., 2012). In view of these superlative properties of CQDs, the CQDs have shown great potential as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of various species (Baig and Chen, 2017; Gunjal et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2013). More importantly, the CQDs possess a very attractive feature of easy functionalisation, which allows further doping heteroatoms to enhance the sensitivity of a CQDs-based nanoprobe for specific species detection. Much evidence have shown that doping heteroatoms, such as nitrogen (N) (Zhai et al., 2012), sulfur (S) (Zhang et al., 2018), boron (B) (Bian et al., 2018), phosphorus (P) (Wang et al., 2014), fluorine (F) (Zuo et al., 2017) and chlorine (Cl) (Zhong et al., 2017), into a CQDs framework could efficiently improve the sensitivity of a CQDs-based nanoprobe. So far, the doped CQDs have been widely used for the detection of various heavy metal ions, such as Fe3+ (Naik et al., 2018), Hg2+ (Xu et al., 2018), Cu2+ (Pawar et al., 2019), Pb2+ (Liu et al., 2019), Ag+ (Gao et al., 2015), Ca2+ (Wang et al., 2015), et al. However, rare efforts have shown the capability of CQDs for the detection of Mn(VII). So far, only one attempt has been made by using CQDs co-doped with N, S and P (N,S,P-CQDS) as a fluorescent nanoprobe for Mn(VII) detection (Gong et al., 2017a). Even though this proposed fluorescence method was quite sensitive with a detection limit of 50 nM was achieved, the synthesis of N,S,P-CQDS lasted for 5 h under hydrothermal heating at 240 ℃, making the nanoprobe fabrication process very time-consuming and energy costive. Therefore, it is highly necessary to search for a more simple, faster and energy-saving synthetic routes to construct more efficient CQDs-based nanoprobe for Mn(VII) determination.

In this study, a S, Cl and N co-doped CQDs (S,Cl,N-CQDs)-based nanoprobe was for the first time developed for the selective and sensitive determination of Mn(VII) (Scheme 1 ). The S,Cl,N-CQDs was readily prepared via a fast, simple and energy-saving acid-base neutralization method with the glucose, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) as the precursors. The dehydration and carbonization of glucose could complete less than 2 min via the neutralization heat with no external energy source was employed. The as-synthesised CQDs exhibits intense blue fluorescence, which could be dramatically quenched by Mn(VII). The optimal sensing conditions (response time, pH and S,Cl,N-CQDs dosage) and the fluorescence quenching mechanism were investigated in detail. The proposed fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied for Mn(VII) detection in food samples with satisfactory results. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on using S,Cl,N-CQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe for Mn(VII) determination in a simple, inexpensive, fast and sensitive way, highlighting the great prospects of CQDs as an efficient fluorescent nanoprobe for Mn(VII) sensing.

All chemicals and reagents used are of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. The potassium permanganate (KMnO4), glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), glucose (Gluc), adenosine (Ade), geniposide (Gen) and glutathione (GSH) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All amino acids, including aspartic acid (Asp), alanine (Ala), leucine (Leu), valine (Val), tryptophan (Try), phenylalanine (Phen), DL-cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), tyrosine (Tyr), glutamate (Glu), threonine (Thr), arginine (Arg), methionine (Met), histidine (His) and serine (Ser), used in this work were obtained from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The food samples used in this work were purchased from the local supermarkets at Yangzhou, Jiangsu province, China. The double distilled water used for solution preparation was purified by a Millipore-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA).

The S,Cl,N-CQDs were fabricated via a simple synthetic procedure. Briefly, 0.4 g glucose, 6.0 mL of EDA, 2.0 mL of H2SO4 solution (70 %) and 2.0 mL of concentrated HCl were quickly mixed in a 100.0 mL glass beaker. The reaction completed within few seconds with the dark brown and foamy solid reaction product was formed, indicating the formation of S,Cl,N-CQDs. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the reaction product was dissolved by double distilled water and transferred into a dialysis membrane tube (MWCO = 500–1000 Da) and dialysed against 1.0 L double distilled water to remove the unreacted precursors and the by-products from the synthetic procedure. The dialysis process was conducted over a course of 3 days with magnetic stirring. The fresh double distilled water was recharged into the beaker every 24 h during the dialysis process. Finally, a clear and dark brown solution was obtained and lyophilized to obtain the dry S,Cl,N-CQDs product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity, selectivity and anti-interference ability study of S,Cl,N-CQDs-based nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The S,Cl,N-CQDs solution was prepared in a 10 mM phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 2.0) at a concentration of 0.075 mg/mL. For the linearity measurement, different amounts of Mn(VII) were added into 2.0 mL S,Cl,N-CQDs solution (0.075 mg/mL) step-wisely, making the final concentrations of Mn(VII) in a range of 0.05–110 μM. Then, the PL intensities of the S,Cl,N-CQDs solution containing different contents of Mn(VII) were recorded at a λex of 380 nm and an emission wavelength (λem) of 466 nm.

For the selectivity measurement, Mn(VII), fifteen kinds of amino acids (Asp, Ala, Leu, Val, Try, Phen, Cys, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Thr, Arg, Met, His and Ser), seventeen kinds of cations (K+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+), eleven kinds of anions (Cl−, I−, Ac−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2-, HCO3 − and CO3 2-) and four kinds of small molecules (Gluc, Ade, Gen and GSH) were mixed with 2.0 mL S,Cl,N-CQDs (0.075 mg/mL) at a concentration of 0.5 mM, respectively. The PL intensities of S,Cl,N-CQDs before and after the addition of the above substances were recorded at a λex of 380 nm and a λem of 466 nm.

For the anti-interference ability measurement, Mn(VII) was firstly mixed with 2.0 mL S,Cl,N-CQDs (0.075 mg/mL) at a concentration of 0.5 mM, and then the above mentioned possible interferences were added to mix at a concentration of 5 mM, respectively. The PL intensities of the S,Cl,N-CQDs/Mn(VII) system before and after the addition of the other potential interferences were recorded at a λex of 380 nm and a λem of 466 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample treatment and analysis by the proposed fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: The food samples analysed include vegetable (onion and purple cabbage), cereal (rice, buckwheat flour and wheat flour) and tea (green tea, red tea, white tea and rose flower tea) samples. The buckwheat flour and wheat flour samples were directly used for further treatment. For the onion, purple cabbage and tea samples, they were cut into small pieces before further treatment. For the rice sample, it was grinded into fine powder before further treatment. Then, each sample (20.0 g of each vegetable sample and 2.0 g of each cereal and tea sample) was mixed with double distilled water and boiled for about 25 min, which afterwards was subjected to centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 15 min. After that, the supernatants containing the sample extractions were collected, filtered through the cellulose acetate syringe filters (0.45 μm, 13 mm id), transferred into 10.0 mL volumetric flasks and diluted to mark with double distilled water. Finally, 20.0 μL of each filtered extractions of the food samples was mixed with 2.0 mL S,Cl,N-CQDs (0.075 mg/mL), respectively. The PL intensities of the mixtures were measured at a λex of 380 nm and a λem of 466 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of food samples by ICP-MS
ce:section.ce:para: The food samples were also analysed by ICP-MS according to the National Standard Method. In detail, 1.0 g of each food sample was mixed with 5.0 mL nitric acid (HNO3) in a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) pressure digestion inner tank. Then, the tank cap was covered tightly and left to stand still overnight. After that, the digestion tank was placed in a drying oven for digestion under programmed temperature (80 °C for 2 h, 120 °C for 2 h and 160 °C for 4 h). After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the digestion tank was taken out and placed on an adjustable temperature-controlled electric heating plate and heated at 140 °C to remove the remaining acid. Finally, the digestion product was diluted to 25 mL with double distilled water for further analysis by a Thermo ICP-MS XII instrument (Thermo Fisher, USA). The conditions of ICP-MS used to determine Mn(VII) in the food samples were present in Table S1.

The morphology and size characterisation of the as-prepared S,Cl,N-CQDs were performed by TEM measurement. As shown by Fig. 1 , the S,Cl,N-CQDs are spherical in structure and are evenly distributed on the TEM grid without obvious agglomeration. To assess the size distribution of the as-prepared S,Cl,N-CQDs, 80 particles at various spots of the TEM image were counted and the corresponding histogram (Inset of Fig. 1) was plotted. It is revealed that the S,Cl,N-CQDs has an average diameter of 3.8 nm with a size deviation of ±0.5 nm.

The structural characterisation of S,Cl,N-CQDs was performed by elemental analysis and FTIR. Table S2 depicts the elemental analysis results of S,Cl,N-CQDs. The as-prepared S,Cl,N-CQDs are composed of C 13.13, H 6.55, N 18.19, Cl 11.21, S 14.60, and O (calculated) 36.32 wt%, respectively, indicating the successful doping of S, Cl, and N into S,Cl,N-CQDs. Fig. S1 displays the FTIR spectrum of S,Cl,N-CQDs. A broad absorption peak at 3321–3537 cm−1is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH bond. The strong absorption peaks at 1352 and 1650 cm−1 are corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C − O and CO bonds, respectively. A weak absorption peak at 2931 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CH bond. In addition, the characteristic absorption peaks ascribed to C − Cl stretching vibration, C − S stretching vibration, C − N stretching vibration and NH bending are also observed at 819, 1140, 1453 and 1527 cm−1, respectively, further confirming that S, Cl, and N have been incorporated into S,Cl,N-CQDs. An attempt was also given to acquire the FTIR spectrum of the carbon source (glucose) for comparison. The characteristic peaks of CH, OH, −CH3 and C − O stretching vibrations are observed. The FTIR information indicates that the heteroatoms S, Cl and N are successfully doped into S,Cl,N-CQDs. In summary, the surface of S,Cl,N-CQDs is covered with carboxylic, amine, amide, alkyl chloride and alkyl sulphide functionalities, which affords the S,Cl,N-CQDs with good hydrophilicity.

The spectral characteristics of S,Cl,N-CQDs were measured by UV–vis (UV–vis) and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 2 A displays the UV–vis absorption spectrum of S,Cl,N-CQDs (blue line). It can be seen that the S,Cl,N-CQDs displays two typical absorption peaks at 280 and 356 nm, which are attributed to the n→π* transition of the CO band and the excited defect surface states induced by S, Cl and N heteroatoms, respectively (Gong et al., 2017b). The excitation spectrum of S,Cl,N-CQDs shows that the S,Cl,N-CQDs displays an excitation peak at 380 nm (red line, Fig. 2A). Upon excitation at 380 nm, the S,Cl,N-CQDs exhibits strong blue emission with an emission band centred at 466 nm (black line, Fig. 2A). In addition, the S,Cl,N-CQDs displays the famous λex-dependent PL behaviour (Fig. 2B); the λem varies in the range of 459 − 573 nm with increasingly longer λex (in 10 nm increments starting from 310 nm). The quantum yield (QY) of the S,Cl,N-CQDs is determined to be 23.3 % by using quinine sulphate as the reference (Fig. S2).

The experimental conditions could not only affect the stability of the sensing system but also affect the sensitivity for Mn(VII) detection. As such, the effect of response time, pH and S,Cl,N-CQDs dosage on the quenching efficiency (I0/I) were investigated in detail. The I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of S,Cl,N-CQDs before and after the addition of 35 μM Mn(VII).

The effect of response time on I0/I was firstly studied. As shown by Fig. 3 A, the I0/I increases when the response time increases from 0 to 3 min, and prolonging response time no longer changes the I0/I value, indicating that the reaction degree between Mn(VII) and S,Cl,N-CQDs reaches stability within 3 min. Consequently, this work selected 3 min as the optimal response time.

Then, the effect of pH on I0/I was investigated. As displayed by Fig. 3B, the I0/I gradually decreases with the pH increasing from 2.0–8.0 and tends to almost constant in the higher pH range (pH ≥ 8). Taking this information into account, the subsequent sensing work selected 2.0 as the optimal pH.

Finally, the effect of S,Cl,N-CQDs dosage on I0/I was examined. As illustrated by Fig. 3C, the I0/I sharply increases with the concentration of S,Cl,N-CQDs increasing from 0.01 to 0.075 mg/mL, and then dramatically decreases when the concentration of S,Cl,N-CQDs is higher than 0.075 mg/mL. Therefore, the subsequent analyses selected 0.075 mg/mL as the optimal S,Cl,N-CQDs dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Mn(VII) by the proposed fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
For the titration experiments, the PL intensities of the S,Cl,N-CQDs solution containing various contents of Mn(VII) were recorded. As shown by Fig. 4 , the PL intensities of S,Cl,N-CQDs sequentially decrease with the increasing of Mn(VII) concentration, indicating that Mn(VII) could efficiently quench the fluorescence of S,Cl,N-CQDs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (I0/I) of S,Cl,N-CQDs was evaluated according to the following Stern-Volmer equation:
I0/I =KSVC+1
where C refers to the Mn(VII) concentration and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The inset in the top-right of Fig. 4 displays the Stern-Volmer plot describing I0/I as a function of Mn(VII) concentration. A good linear relationship is observed in a wide concentration range of 0.05–110 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9992, and the KSV is calculated to be 7 × 104 L/mol. The detection limit for Mn(VII) detection is determined to be 12.8 nM according to 3 times the standard deviation of the blank rule (3σ/slope, n = 12 independent measurements).

The analytical characteristics of the proposed fluorescence method are compared to the previously reported methods. As shown by Table 1 , the fluorescence method proposed in this work achieved much higher sensitivity than the previously reported spectrophotometry and CQDs-based spectrofluorimetry methods. Particularly, the sensitivity of our proposed sensing system is even higher than or comparable to some advanced modern instrumental methods, such as ICP-OES, AAS and FAAS methods. When compared to the ICP-MS method, even though the sensitivity obtained by this work is not as high as that of the ICP-MS method, our proposed sensing system provides merits of great simplicity, fast in analysis and cost-effectiveness. In addition, our proposed fluorescence method achieved a wider linear range than any of the previously reported method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed sensing system was examined by studying the fluorescence response of the sensing system towards Mn(VII) and other possible interfering substances. In detail, Mn(VII), fifteen kinds of amino acids (Asp, Ala, Leu, Val, Try, Phen, Cys, Gly, Tyr, Glu, Thr, Arg, Met, His and Ser), seventeen kinds of cations (K+, Li+, Na+, Pb2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ni3+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+), eleven kinds of anions (Cl−, I−, Ac−, NO3 −, NO2 −, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2-, HCO3 − and CO3 2-) and four kinds of small molecules (Gluc, Ade, Gen and GSH) were mixed with a S,Cl,N-CQDs solution (0.075 mg/mL), respectively, and the concentration of each species was set at 0.5 mM. The PL intensities of S,Cl,N-CQDs before and after the addition of the other possible interfering substances were recorded. As shown by Fig. 5 A, only Mn(VII) causes the enormous quenching of the PL intensity of S,Cl,N-CQDs whereas the other possible interfering substances lead to slight or even no change of the PL intensity of S,Cl,N-CQDs. This indicates that the S,Cl,N-CQDs have good selectivity toward Mn(VII) over the other competitive substances.

The anti-interference ability of the proposed sensing system was investigated by studying the impacts of the possible interfering substances on Mn(VII) sensing. The fluorescence response of the sensing system towards Mn(VII) (0.5 mM) in the absence and in the presence of the same concentration of other possible interfering substances is depicted in Fig. 5B. It can be seen that the coexistence of above-mentioned competitive coexisting substances does not interfere with the determination of Mn(VII). Further attempts were given to study the tolerance of foreign species in the determination of 0.5 mM of Mn(VII). As shown by Table S3, most species could be allowed to coexist at a concentration of >4-fold of Mn(VII). Specifically, with the amino acids, five metal ions (K+, Na+, Li+, Mn2+, Fe2+), five anion ions (NO3 −, NO2 − Cl−, SO4 2-, SO3 2-), and four kinds of small molecules (Gluc, Ade, Gen, GSH) could be tolerated at 30, 20, 10 and 80-fold of the Mn(VII) concentration, respectively. The results clearly indicate that the S,Cl,N-CQDs possesses high anti-interference ability toward Mn(VII) sensing, which affords the potential feasibility of the proposed sensing system for real sample analysis.

To study the sensing mechanism for Mn(VII) detection, the attempt was firstly given to study the spectrum characteristics of Mn(VII) and S,Cl,N-CQDs. Fig. 6 A (purple line) displays the absorption spectrum of Mn(VII). It can be seen that Mn(VII) displays typical absorption peaks at 230, 310, 350, 486, 505, 525, 545 and 568 nm, respectively. The excitation spectrum of S,Cl,N-CQDs (black line, Fig. 6A) shows that the S,Cl,N-CQDs has an excitation peak at 380 nm. Upon excitation at 380 nm, the S,Cl,N-CQDs displays an emission peak at 466 nm (blue line, Fig. 6A). It is revealed that the absorption spectrum of Mn(VII) shows a good overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of S,Cl,N-CQDs, i.e., Mn(VII) could shield excitation and emission light from S,Cl,N-CQDs. This indicates that the inner filter effect (IFE) occurred between S,Cl,N-CQDs and Mn(VII) with the S,Cl,N-CQDs as a fluorophore and the Mn(VII) as a light absorber.

To further understand the interactions between the S,Cl,N-CQDs fluorophore and the Mn(VII) absorber, the fluorescence decay curves of S,Cl,N-CQDs before and after the addition of Mn(VII) were acquired and are depicted in Fig. 6B. The corresponding lifetimes are determined by fitting the decay curves with the double-exponential function (Electronic Supplementary Material), and the relevant fitting data are displayed in Table S4. Before the addition of Mn(VII), the S,Cl,N-CQDs have an average lifetime of 7.01 ns (τ1 = 2.21 ns (25.82 %) and τ2 = 8.68 ns (74.18 %)). After Mn(VII) is added, the average lifetime of S,Cl,N-CQDs is decreased to 3.44 ns (τ1 = 1.09 ns (26.70 %) and τ2 = 4.29 ns (73.30 %)). The decrease of the S,Cl,N-CQDs lifetime by Mn(VII) implies that the fluorescence quenching is a dynamic quenching process rather than a static process. Overall, the interaction between S,Cl,N-CQDs and Mn(VII) is a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching.

The S,Cl,N-CQDs-based sensing system was utilised for the detection of Mn(VII) in food samples, including vegetable (onion and purple cabbage), cereal (rice, buckwheat flour and wheat flour) and tea (green tea, red tea, white tea and rose flower tea) samples. As shown by Table 2 , the contents of Mn(VII) in the extractions of onion, purple cabbage, rice, buckwheat flour, wheat flour, green tea, red tea, white tea and rose flower tea are 0.26, 0.80, 0.36, 0.98, 0.19, 18.41, 14.56, 30.14 and 0.74 μM, respectively. Therefore, the original contents of Mn(VII) in the above food samples are 0.74, 2.24, 10.20, 27.52, 5.28, 514.85, 407.26, 843.15 and 20.61 mg/kg, respectively. The spike recoveries obtained for the determination of Mn(VII) in the above food samples are in the range of 98.32 − 104.03 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 0.13 and 5.64 % (n = 5).

The contents of Mn(VII) in the above food samples were also determined by ICP-MS method in order to check whether the results obtained from the proposed fluorescence method are accurate. The experiments were conducted exactly according to the national standard method and the analysis results are shown in Table 2. In addition, the t-test (Table S5) was carried out by using a SPSS 21.0 software to check if there is significant difference between the results achieved by the two methods. It can be seen that the results achieved by the two methods do not have significant difference, which means that the results obtained by our work are in good consistence with that achieved by ICP-MS. This unambiguously demonstrates that our proposed fluorescence method is of high accuracy for Mn(VII) detection in food matrix.

In this work, a S,Cl,N-CQDs-based fluorescent nanoprobe was for the first time developed for the determination of Mn(VII) in a highly selective and sensitive way. The fluorescence of S,Cl,N-CQDs could be dramatically quenched by Mn(VII) and the quenching mechanism is revealed to be a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching. The proposed analytical method achieved a wide linear detection range of 0.05–110 μM and a detection limit down to 12.8 nM, which are better than or comparable to most of the previously established analytical methods. Furthermore, the proposed sensing system was successfully used for the detection of Mn(VII) in vegetable, cereal and tea samples with satisfactory results are achieved. The proposed fluorescence method provides many advantages, such as good selectivity, high sensitivity, simplicity, fast in analysis and cost-effectiveness. In view of these merits, the as-constructed S,Cl,N-CQDs host great prospects in constructing a nanoprobe device for the highly efficient determination of Mn(VII) in complex food matrix, and more detailed efforts can now follow. For example, taking advantages of the easy functionalisation feature of CQDs, the fluorescence properties of CQDs could be further optimised or tuned by doping other heteroatoms into CQDs in order to further advance the analytical performance of the CQDs-based sensing method. Further, the signal amplification strategy by forming CQDs-based nanospheres could be a potential pathway to greatly enhance detection sensitivity and reduce the consumption of CQDs amount, which would certainly benefit the construction of a nanoprobe device. This is work in progress currently underway in our laboratory. Finally, most of the reported synthetic procedure for CQDs is still in small-scale, and the ability to scale-up the synthesis is a particularly attractive prospect to meet the construction requirement of a nanoprobe device.
Q.H., X.G., and Z.Y. designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Q.H., L-f.L., and H.S. performed the experiments. Q.H., L-f.L., and L.L. analysed the data.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely thank the financial supports from Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202, 21705101 and 31371806), Foundation of China National Key R&D Program (2016YFC1300201), China KFC Catering Health Fund (2017), Opening Foundation of Jiangsu Huaiyang Cuisine Industrialization Engineering Center (2017) and Yangzhou University Science and Technology Innovation Team (2016) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2020.103447.
